To keep the empirical workflow fully reproducible and consistent with the package interface, we use the processed object `cop_oil_postshock` distributed with the package rather than reconstructing the windows manually in the main text. This object contains the aligned target and donor shock windows, the corresponding macro-financial covariate matrices, and the shock timing metadata required by the forecasting functions.

In particular, `cop_oil_postshock` stores the outcome series in `Y`, the aligned covariate matrices in `X`, the covariate names in `covar_names`, and the local shock timing information in `shock_time_vec` and `shock_length_vec`. The empirical analysis therefore follows the same workflow as the general package implementation and can be reproduced directly from the processed object.

Using the same processed object, we next examine whether separating donor episodes into multiple pools provides a more interpretable or more accurate postshock adjustment for the March 2020 COP episode.

```{r cop-empirical-call, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, cache=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
data("cop_oil_postshock")

out_cop <- SynthPrediction(
  Y_series_list          = cop_oil_postshock$Y,
  covariates_series_list = cop_oil_postshock$X,
  shock_time_vec         = cop_oil_postshock$shock_time_vec,
  shock_length_vec       = cop_oil_postshock$shock_length_vec,
  k                      = 1,
  covariate_indices      = NULL,
  seasonal               = TRUE,
  use_dbw                = TRUE,
  plots                  = FALSE
)

forecast_df <- data.frame(
  Method = c("Actual", "Unadjusted", "Arithmetic mean", "DBW adjusted"),
  Value  = c(
    as.numeric(cop_oil_postshock$Y[[1]][cop_oil_postshock$shock_time_vec[1] + 1]),
    as.numeric(out_cop$predictions$unadjusted),
    as.numeric(out_cop$predictions$arithmetic_mean),
    as.numeric(out_cop$predictions$adjusted)
  )
)

knitr::kable(
  forecast_df,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Forecast comparison for the March 2020 COP shock episode."
)

weight_df <- data.frame(
  Donor = names(cop_oil_postshock$Y)[-1],
  Weight = as.numeric(out_cop$linear_combinations)
)

knitr::kable(
  weight_df,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "DBW donor weights for the COP empirical application."
)

target_y <- as.numeric(cop_oil_postshock$Y[[1]])
shock_t0 <- cop_oil_postshock$shock_time_vec[1]
pred_day <- shock_t0 + 1

plot_df <- data.frame(
  Day = 1:pred_day,
  COP = target_y[1:pred_day]
)

forecast_points <- data.frame(
  Day = c(pred_day, pred_day - 0.15, pred_day + 0.15, pred_day + 0.30),
  Method = factor(
    c("Actual", "Unadjusted", "Arithmetic mean", "DBW adjusted"),
    levels = c("Actual", "Unadjusted", "Arithmetic mean", "DBW adjusted")
  ),
  Value = c(
    as.numeric(target_y[pred_day]),
    as.numeric(out_cop$predictions$unadjusted),
    as.numeric(out_cop$predictions$arithmetic_mean),
    as.numeric(out_cop$predictions$adjusted)
  )
)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = plot_df, aes(x = Day, y = COP), color = "black") +
  geom_point(data = plot_df, aes(x = Day, y = COP), color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = pred_day, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(
    data = forecast_points,
    aes(x = Day, y = Value, color = Method, shape = Method),
    size = 3
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Actual" = "black",
    "Unadjusted" = "red",
    "Arithmetic mean" = "blue",
    "DBW adjusted" = "darkgreen"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "COP path up to the forecast date with postshock forecast comparison",
    x = "Day in local shock window",
    y = "COP price"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## MultiPool extension

### Motivation

The single-pool donor adjustment treats all historical donor episodes as belonging to a common donor set. In the COP application, however, this may be restrictive because the March 2020 collapse plausibly reflected multiple channels, including oil-market supply disruptions and broader demand or financial-stress effects. A MultiPool extension allows these channels to be represented separately by grouping donor episodes into economically interpretable pools and aggregating their contributions afterwards.

### Pool definition

Using the same COP shock windows as in the baseline empirical analysis, we partition the donor episodes into two pools. The late-November 2014 episode is assigned to a **supply** pool because it is naturally associated with the OPEC-related oil-price collapse. The March and September 2008 episodes are assigned to a **demand** pool, as they are more closely associated with crisis-driven market stress and demand contraction.

Formally, the pool structure is specified as
\[
\texttt{pools} =
\{\texttt{supply} = (\texttt{Y\_2014\_11\_28}),\;
\texttt{demand} = (\texttt{Y\_2008\_03\_17}, \texttt{Y\_2008\_09\_09}, \texttt{Y\_2008\_09\_15}, \texttt{Y\_2008\_09\_29})\}.
\]

### MultiPool implementation

We apply the MultiPool version of `SynthPrediction()` using the same aligned COP outcome and covariate series as in the baseline case. In this experiment, donor balancing weights are applied within each pool (`pool_use_dbw = TRUE`), while the baseline target forecast is kept as the unweighted ARIMA/ARIMAX component (`base_use_dbw = FALSE`).

We consider two aggregation rules across pools:

1. **Sum aggregation** (`pool_agg = "sum"`), which adds the estimated pool-level shock effects;
2. **Weighted aggregation** (`pool_agg = "weighted"`), which combines the pool-level shock effects using user-specified pool weights.

This comparison allows us to assess whether separating supply-side and demand-side donor pools yields a more interpretable or more accurate postshock adjustment than the single-pool specification.
```{r cop-multipool-call, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, cache=TRUE}
library(knitr)

pre_len  <- 30L
post_len <- 1L
k <- 1L

Y_series_list          <- cop_oil_postshock$Y
covariates_series_list <- cop_oil_postshock$X

shock_time_vec   <- rep(pre_len,  length(Y_series_list))
shock_length_vec <- rep(post_len, length(Y_series_list))

pools <- list(
  supply = c("Y_2014_11_28"),
  demand = c("Y_2008_03_17", "Y_2008_09_09", "Y_2008_09_15", "Y_2008_09_29")
)

# MultiPool with additive aggregation
out_sum <- SynthPrediction(
  Y_series_list          = Y_series_list,
  covariates_series_list = covariates_series_list,
  shock_time_vec         = shock_time_vec,
  shock_length_vec       = shock_length_vec,
  k                      = k,
  pools                  = pools,
  pool_agg               = "sum",
  base_use_dbw           = FALSE,
  pool_use_dbw           = TRUE,
  plots                  = FALSE
)

# MultiPool with weighted aggregation
out_wsum <- SynthPrediction(
  Y_series_list          = Y_series_list,
  covariates_series_list = covariates_series_list,
  shock_time_vec         = shock_time_vec,
  shock_length_vec       = shock_length_vec,
  k                      = k,
  pools                  = pools,
  pool_agg               = "weighted",
  pool_weights           = c(supply = 0.9, demand = 0.9),
  base_use_dbw           = FALSE,
  pool_use_dbw           = TRUE,
  plots                  = FALSE
)

multipool_df <- data.frame(
  Method = c("Actual", "Unadjusted", "Single-pool DBW", "MultiPool (sum)", "MultiPool (weighted)"),
  Value  = c(
    as.numeric(cop_oil_postshock$Y[[1]][pre_len + 1]),
    as.numeric(out_cop$predictions$unadjusted),
    as.numeric(out_cop$predictions$adjusted),
    as.numeric(out_sum$predictions$adjusted_multipool),
    as.numeric(out_wsum$predictions$adjusted_multipool)
  )
)

knitr::kable(
  multipool_df,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Forecast comparison for the COP empirical application under MultiPool specifications."
)
target_y <- as.numeric(cop_oil_postshock$Y[[1]])
pred_day <- pre_len + 1L

path_df <- data.frame(
  Day = seq_len(pred_day),
  Price = target_y[seq_len(pred_day)]
)

forecast_df <- data.frame(
  Day = c(pred_day, pred_day + 0.10, pred_day + 0.20, pred_day + 0.30, pred_day + 0.40),
  Method = factor(
    c("Actual", "Unadjusted", "Single-pool DBW", "MultiPool (sum)", "MultiPool (weighted)"),
    levels = c("Actual", "Unadjusted", "Single-pool DBW", "MultiPool (sum)", "MultiPool (weighted)")
  ),
  Value = c(
    as.numeric(cop_oil_postshock$Y[[1]][pred_day]),
    as.numeric(out_cop$predictions$unadjusted),
    as.numeric(out_cop$predictions$adjusted),
    as.numeric(out_sum$predictions$adjusted_multipool),
    as.numeric(out_wsum$predictions$adjusted_multipool)
  )
)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = path_df,
    aes(x = Day, y = Price),
    color = "grey55",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = path_df,
    aes(x = Day, y = Price),
    color = "black",
    size = 2
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = forecast_df,
    aes(x = Day, y = Value, color = Method, shape = Method),
    size = 3.2
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Actual" = "black",
    "Unadjusted" = "#F4A3A3",
    "Single-pool DBW" = "#8DB6F2",
    "MultiPool (sum)" = "#9ED9A3",
    "MultiPool (weighted)" = "#C8A2E8"
  )) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(
    "Actual" = 16,
    "Unadjusted" = 17,
    "Single-pool DBW" = 15,
    "MultiPool (sum)" = 18,
    "MultiPool (weighted)" = 3
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "COP path up to the forecast date under single-pool and MultiPool specifications",
    x = "Day in local shock window",
    y = "COP price"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Summary and discussion

In this empirical section we applied the postshock forecasting framework to the ConocoPhillips data set using the processed package object `cop_oil_postshock`. The COVID-19 oil-price collapse of 9 March 2020 was treated as the target episode, while five earlier crisis windows from 2008 and 2014 served as donor episodes. The packaged object contains the aligned target and donor outcome series, the corresponding macro-financial covariate matrices, and the associated shock metadata, allowing the empirical workflow to be reproduced directly through the package interface.

The baseline empirical results illustrate the contribution of donor information relative to the unadjusted ARIMAX forecast. The unadjusted forecast, obtained by extrapolating the pre-shock target dynamics without any donor-based correction, lies substantially above the realised COP price on the shock date. Both the arithmetic-mean adjustment and the DBW-adjusted forecast move closer to the realised value, with the DBW-adjusted version performing slightly better in this case. This supports the idea that reweighting donor episodes according to pre-shock similarity can improve postshock prediction relative to simple averaging.

The MultiPool extension further enriches the empirical analysis by allowing donor episodes to be partitioned into economically interpretable groups. In the COP application, the donor episodes were separated into supply-related and demand-related pools, reflecting the view that the March 2020 collapse likely combined multiple transmission channels. The MultiPool results show that this extension can provide a more structured and interpretable decomposition of the postshock adjustment, while also yielding forecasts that are competitive with, and in this case slightly closer to the realised value than, the single-pool DBW forecast.

Overall, the COP case study demonstrates that the proposed framework can transfer information from historical shock episodes to a new target event in a reproducible and modular way. The same package workflow can be adapted to alternative donor structures, additional pooling rules, or other financial and macroeconomic time series, making the framework flexible for a broad class of postshock forecasting problems.


# Empirical Extension: Apple in the Presence of Control Shocks

In this section we consider an Apple (AAPL) example designed to illustrate the control-shock extension of the postshock forecasting framework. Unlike the ConocoPhillips case, where the main focus was on transferring information across donor shock episodes and on decomposing donor structure through the MultiPool specification, the Apple example is intended to show how the framework can be adapted when the postshock movement of interest may be confounded by additional market-wide or background shocks.

## Motivation and empirical setting

Apple provides a useful case study because product-release events often generate stock-price movements that are plausibly mixed with broader market conditions. In such settings, the observed postshock movement in the target series may reflect both the event-specific shock of interest and an additional control component that is not unique to the target event. This motivates a control-shock formulation, in which the forecasting procedure seeks to separate the event-specific adjustment from more general background movements.

Following the validation design used in the Apple study, we treat one Apple shock window as the target episode and use several earlier Apple-related windows as donor episodes. The donor windows are selected to capture similar event environments while still allowing heterogeneity in the realised postshock responses. As in the COP application, all windows are locally aligned and inflation-adjusted, and the analysis is carried out on trading-day windows centered around the event date.

## Empirical implementation using the packaged data object

To keep the Apple validation study reproducible and aligned with the package interface, we use the processed object `aapl_control_input` distributed with the package. This object contains the aligned target and donor outcome series, the corresponding macro-financial covariate matrices, and the associated local shock timing information required by the synthetic forecasting procedure.

In particular, `aapl_control_input` stores the outcome series in `Y`, the aligned covariate matrices in `X`, the covariate names in `covar_names`, and the local shock metadata in `shock_time_vec` and `shock_length_vec`. Thus, the Apple analysis can be implemented directly from the packaged object without reconstructing the event windows manually in the main text.

In the control-shock experiment, we supplement these packaged inputs with an explicit control-shock specification within the target window and then pass all components to `SynthPrediction()`. The adjusted forecast reported below is therefore obtained under the control-shock setup, while the unadjusted forecast is the corresponding benchmark based only on the pre-shock target dynamics.

```{r aapl-controlshock-call, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, cache=TRUE}
library(knitr)
library(ggplot2)

data("aapl_control_input")

Y_series_list          <- aapl_control_input$Y
covariates_series_list <- aapl_control_input$X
shock_time_vec         <- aapl_control_input$shock_time_vec
shock_length_vec       <- aapl_control_input$shock_length_vec

# control shock specification for the target window
# replace this with the exact local index you want to use
control_shocks <- list(
  list(time = 10L, length = 1L, shape = "point")
)

out_aapl <- SynthPrediction(
  Y_series_list            = Y_series_list,
  covariates_series_list   = covariates_series_list,
  shock_time_vec           = shock_time_vec,
  shock_length_vec         = shock_length_vec,
  k                        = 1,
  use_dbw                  = TRUE,
  dbw_indices              = 1:2,
  covariate_indices        = NULL,
  seasonal                 = FALSE,
  control_shocks           = control_shocks,
  dbw_center               = TRUE,
  dbw_scale                = TRUE,
  days_before_shocktime_vec = 10L,
  plots                    = FALSE
)

actual_aapl <- as.numeric(
  Y_series_list[[1]][shock_time_vec[1] + 1]
)

aapl_result_df <- data.frame(
  Method = c("Actual", "Unadjusted", "Adjusted"),
  Value  = c(
    actual_aapl,
    as.numeric(out_aapl$predictions$unadjusted),
    as.numeric(out_aapl$predictions$adjusted)
  )
)

knitr::kable(
  aapl_result_df,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Forecast comparison for the Apple control-shock experiment."
)

aapl_donor_names <- c("2017-06-06", "2018-10-30", "2019-06-03")
aapl_weights <- as.numeric(out_aapl$meta$weights)

aapl_weight_df <- data.frame(
  Donor  = aapl_donor_names[seq_along(aapl_weights)],
  Weight = aapl_weights
)

knitr::kable(
  aapl_weight_df,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "DBW donor weights for the Apple control-shock experiment."
)
```

 
```{r aapl-plot-data, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, cache=TRUE}
# target path
aapl_target_y <- as.numeric(Y_series_list[[1]])
aapl_n <- length(aapl_target_y)

# actual value at the forecast horizon
actual_aapl <- as.numeric(
  Y_series_list[[1]][shock_time_vec[1] + 1]
)

# predictions
aapl_unadjusted <- as.numeric(out_aapl$predictions$unadjusted)
aapl_adjusted   <- as.numeric(out_aapl$predictions$adjusted)

# main path data
aapl_path_df <- data.frame(
  Day = seq_len(aapl_n),
  Price = aapl_target_y
)

# forecast points at the horizon
aapl_forecast_df <- data.frame(
  Day = c(
    shock_time_vec[1] + 1,
    shock_time_vec[1] + 1.10,
    shock_time_vec[1] + 1.20
  ),
  Method = factor(
    c("True value", "Unadjusted", "Adjusted"),
    levels = c("True value", "Unadjusted", "Adjusted")
  ),
  Value = c(
    actual_aapl,
    aapl_unadjusted,
    aapl_adjusted
  )
)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = aapl_path_df,
    aes(x = Day, y = Price),
    color = "#e78ac3",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = aapl_path_df,
    aes(x = Day, y = Price),
    color = "black",
    size = 1.8
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = aapl_forecast_df,
    aes(x = Day, y = Value, color = Method, shape = Method),
    size = 3
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "True value" = "black",
    "Unadjusted" = "#e78ac3",
    "Adjusted" = "#1f78b4"
  )) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(
    "True value" = 16,
    "Unadjusted" = 16,
    "Adjusted" = 3
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Apple target window with postshock forecast comparison",
    x = "Day",
    y = "Closing stock price"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
## Results

The Apple example is used to illustrate the control-shock version of the forecasting framework. Using the processed object `aapl_control_input`, we construct the target and donor windows, specify the local shock timing information, and then estimate the postshock forecast under a control-shock specification.

In this implementation, the reported adjusted forecast already incorporates the control-shock structure through the `control_shocks` argument in `SynthPrediction()`. Accordingly, the empirical comparison focuses on three quantities: the realised postshock value, the unadjusted forecast based only on pre-shock target dynamics, and the adjusted forecast obtained after incorporating donor-based information under the control-shock setup.

In this example, the realised postshock Apple price is 119.490, compared with an unadjusted forecast of 116.108 and an adjusted forecast of 117.193. Thus, the adjusted forecast moves closer to the realised value than the unadjusted forecast, although some underprediction remains. This indicates that the framework remains useful in settings where the observed postshock movement is interpreted in the presence of an additional control component, rather than as a purely isolated target shock.



# Conclusion

This technical report developed a modular postshock forecasting framework for time series with structural breaks in both mean and volatility. The framework combines donor-based adjustment, synthetic weighting, and flexible extensions for more complex empirical settings.

The simulation studies show that donor-based adjustment improves postshock forecasting relative to unadjusted benchmarks, and that the DBW mechanism is especially useful when donor episodes differ in their pre-shock covariate environments. In the volatility setting, the framework also extends naturally to postshock variance prediction through GARCHX-type modeling.

The empirical case studies highlight two complementary forms of flexibility. In the ConocoPhillips application, the framework improves upon the unadjusted benchmark and can be further enriched through the MultiPool extension, which groups donor episodes into economically interpretable channels. In the Apple example, the control-shock formulation shows that the same forecasting core can also be applied when the observed postshock response is interpreted in the presence of an additional background component.

Taken together, these results show that the package is not limited to a single fixed application template. Instead, it supports a reproducible and modular workflow that can be adapted to alternative donor structures, different economic mechanisms, and richer interpretations of postshock behavior. Possible future work includes broader real-data volatility applications, additional pooling schemes, and further extensions of the control-shock framework.


# References

Lin, Jilei, and Daniel J. Eck (2021). “Minimizing post-shock forecasting error through
aggregation of outside information.” *International Journal of Forecasting*,
37(4), 1710–1727. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijforecast.2021.03.010

Sucarrat, Genaro (2021). “garchx: Flexible and Robust GARCH-X Modelling.”
*The R Journal*, 13(1), 276–291.
